input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A cyber-physical asset within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, such as a building, characterized by complex and heterogeneous data sources requiring integration of static models with real-time operational data for simulation, analysis, and optimization across the entire lifecycle from design to operation and refurbishment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers services including real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, simulation, data analysis, and optimization to support AECO stakeholders in decision-making and collaboration throughout the building's lifecycle from initial design through operation and refurbishment.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system maintains real-time synchronization between physical assets and the virtual representation through continuous data exchange, enabling real-time monitoring and analysis. This synchronization is critical for providing immediate insights and supporting dynamic decision-making processes in the AECO domain.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system employs a model-driven framework that integrates Eclipse Hono for IoT data ingestion and Ditto for digital twin management, utilizing Unreal Engine for high-fidelity visualization. The architecture is modular with defined extension points, and the DT Framework acts as a central hub coordinating all components, ensuring seamless orchestration and system-wide coordination.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are based on Building Information Modeling (BIM) enriched with sensors, actuators, and controllers, providing a unified semantic foundation. Data sources include BIM models processed through Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations and generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real-time sensor data from physical assets is synchronized with the digital twin using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, with continuous data streams processed through metadata (tenant ID, device endpoint addresses) for context-aware integration and quality assurance.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control signals transmitted from the digital twin to physical systems via bidirectional data exchange, allowing Facility Managers to monitor and adjust building operations in real time.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, and runtime configuration management enabling adjustments without full redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The framework leverages Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable Digital Twin artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Its modular architecture features standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, enabling evolution of the Digital Twin in response to operational changes.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately synthesizes the definition of the Digital Twin system in the AECO domain described in the introduction. It correctly identifies the system as a cyber-physical asset (e.g., a building) requiring the integration of static models with real-time data for simulation, analysis, and optimization across the lifecycle. This is supported by the text which states DTs are 'cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization' and operate along 'the complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments'.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific services provided by the Digital Twin, including real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, simulation, and optimization. It also accurately describes the purpose (supporting stakeholders in decision-making and collaboration) and the scope (building lifecycle). Evidence from the text includes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization of building operations' and 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'.
The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly mentions specific tools and technologies such as Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine, and BIM.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic correctly identifies the system's use of real-time synchronization and continuous data exchange. The source documents explicitly mention 'real-time data exchange', 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins', and the need to ensure 'continuous synchronization'. While the extraction captures these core concepts, it is slightly less detailed regarding specific technical mechanisms (e.g., latency) than the source allows, warranting a 'Good' score.

The document describes a model-driven framework for generating individual digital twins and discusses modular services within a platform. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures of twins, or decentralized architectures involving coordination between distinct twins.

The description provides a highly specific and accurate summary of the system's architecture and orchestration. It correctly lists the integration of 'Eclipse Hono' and 'Ditto', the use of 'Unreal Engine', the 'modular' nature with 'standardized extension points', and the role of the 'DT Framework' as a 'central hub'. This is strongly grounded in the provided text.

While the document mentions 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' and 'standardized extension points', it focuses primarily on the vertical integration of BIM models with runtime data (IoT) and internal components (Hono, Ditto). It does not provide specific details on integration patterns, data exchange protocols, or dependencies with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description accurately synthesizes the model-driven approach described in the text. It correctly identifies BIM as the foundation enriched with devices and details the MDE transformation pipeline. Quotes supporting this include: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces', and 'The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts'.

States 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions actuators as a category of devices to be modeled ('define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'), it does not provide specific details about the physical actuators themselves, such as types, actuation ranges, safety constraints, or specific control capabilities required by the characteristic description.

States 'Not in Document'. The text references sensors generally in the context of the framework requirements and case studies ('post-occupancy sensor data', 'sensor configurations'), but it does not list specific sensor types, measurement capabilities, spatial distribution, or data transmission specifications.

The documents focus on deployment framework architecture and MDE workflows rather than model fidelity, validation metrics, or uncertainty quantification. The only potential reference to validation is a software library citation [65] for model validation. Therefore, the statement 'Not in Document' is accurate.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the synchronization of sensor data using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, and correctly lists specific metadata types (tenant ID, device endpoint addresses) found in the text. It captures the concept of 'continuous synchronization' and 'real-time data integration' well. However, the mention of 'quality assurance' is not explicitly supported by the source text, though 'semantic integration' is mentioned.

The extraction accurately describes the bidirectional nature of the data exchange and the role of the Facility Manager in controlling the infrastructure. Supported by quotes: 'bidirectional communication between virtual and physical assets' and 'Facility Managers... operates the physical infrastructure via the DT'.

The characteristic description asks for specific network infrastructure details such as bandwidth, latency, network topology, and security protocols. The provided text mentions Eclipse Hono and Ditto for connectivity but does not provide the specific technical network infrastructure details required by the definition. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is a correct assessment.

The extraction accurately reflects the modular architecture and configuration management capabilities described. It correctly cites the extension points and the ability to adjust configurations without full redeployment. Supported by quotes: 'modular architecture with defined extension points' and 'Runtime configuration management... enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
